subject: Gastroenterology
title: Bowel obstruction
preambleMarkdown:
# Bowel obstruction

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Bowel obstruction is a clinical condition or syndrome in gastroenterology.
- This article focuses on GI anatomy, motility, inflammation, bleeding, obstruction, liver-pancreas-biliary disease, and nutrition.
- The practical clinical priority is gastroenterology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Bowel obstruction to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Bowel obstruction is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is gastroenterology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Bowel obstruction depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In gastroenterology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Bowel obstruction.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Bowel obstruction disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Bowel obstruction should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Bowel obstruction first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Bowel obstruction, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Bowel obstruction, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused gastroenterology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Bowel obstruction presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Bowel obstruction with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Bowel obstruction is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Bowel obstruction should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Bowel obstruction is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses gastroenterology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Bowel obstruction, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Bowel obstruction may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Bowel obstruction patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Bowel obstruction, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Bowel obstruction in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Bowel obstruction, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Bowel obstruction does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Bowel obstruction may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Bowel obstruction, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Bowel obstruction as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Bowel obstruction draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

### Source Excerpt: Small Bowel Obstruction

_Source lines: 14562-14683._

```text
Small Bowel Obstruction
A. General Characteristics
1. There are three main points of differentiation to consider in small
bowel obstruction (SBO).

a. Partial versus complete obstruction
- With partial obstruction, patients are able to pass gas or
have bowel movements, as opposed to complete obstruction
- However, patients with complete obstruction may
occasionally be able to pass gas or stool because they may
have residual stool or gas in the colon
d. Closed-loop versus open-loop obstruction
- With closed-loop obstruction, the lumen is occluded at two
points by an adhesive band or hernia ring. This can
compromise the blood supply, requiring emergent surgery
f. Proximal versus distal SBO
- Distal obstruction causes distention of proximal bowel
segments, making diagnosis easier on plain radiograph

## 2. Pathophysiology

a. Dehydration is a key event in SBO. Intestinal distention
causes reflex vomiting, increased intestinal secretion proximal
to the point of obstruction, and decreased absorption. This leads
to hypochloremia, hypokalemia, and metabolic alkalosis
b. The resulting hypovolemia leads to systemic findings such as
tachycardia, hypotension, tachypnea, altered mental status, and
oliguria
Quick HIT
SBO
- Proximal obstruction: frequent vomiting, severe pain, minimal
abdominal distention
- Distal obstruction: less frequent vomiting and significant
abdominal distention
Quick HIT
Excessively high intraluminal pressure may compromise blood

supply, leading to strangulation. This can lead to shock, gangrene,
peritonitis, or perforation of bowel-all devastating complications.

Quick HIT
Large Bowel Obstruction
- Causes: colon cancer (most common cause), volvulus,
adhesions, hernias, strictures
- Results in less fluid and electrolyte disorders than SBO
- See Figure 3-13 for radiographic findings
B. Causes
1. Adhesions from previous abdominal surgery-most common cause
in adults.

2. Incarcerated hernias-second most common cause.

3. Malignancy (including peritoneal carcinomatosis), intussusception,
Crohn disease, and superior mesenteric artery syndrome
(compression of third portion of duodenum).

FIGURE
3-13 A: An AP supine film of large bowel obstruction. Note the dilated air-
filled proximal colon with an absence of air in the distal colon. B: An AP
upright film of large bowel obstruction. Note multiple colon air-fluid levels
(curved arrow).

(Reprinted with permission from Erkonen WE, Smith WL. Radiology 101: The Basics and
Fundamentals of Imaging. Lippincott-Raven; 1998:158. Figure 8-21A and B.)

C. Clinical Features
1. Acute onset cramping abdominal pain-if pain is continuous and
severe, strangulation or peritonitis from perforation may be present
2. Nausea, vomiting-may be feculent
3. Obstipation (absence of stool and flatus)
4. Abdominal distention
5. Signs of hypovolemia (tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension)
D. Diagnosis
1. Abdominal plain films (upright or lateral decubitus and supine
films)-dilated loops of small bowel, air-fluid levels proximal to
point of obstruction (on upright film), and minimal gas in colon (if
complete SBO) (Figure 3-14).

2. CT abdomen and pelvis-to evaluate severity, location, and
potential complications of obstruction. IV and PO contrast are ideal
(omit PO contrast if suspect complete obstruction).

Quick HIT
Manifestations of bowel compromise in SBO include fever, severe
and continuous pain, peritoneal signs, SIRS, gas in the bowel wall
or portal vein, abdominal free air, and metabolic acidosis.

E. Treatment
1. Nonoperative management-appropriate if bowel obstruction is
incomplete and there is no fever, tachycardia, peritoneal signs, or
leukocytosis. Surgical consultation is always indicated.

a. Bowel rest (NPO)
b. IV fluids to establish adequate urine output; replete electrolyte
abnormalities (usually require potassium repletion)
c. Nasogastric tube to empty stomach (gastric decompression)
2. Surgery is indicated for complete obstruction, closed-loop
obstruction, failure of nonoperative management, or if bowel
compromise (ischemia, necrosis, or perforation) is suspected.

Perform an exploratory laparotomy with lysis of adhesions and
resection of any necrotic bowel

FIGURE
3-14 A: An AP supine film of small bowel obstruction shows prominent
valvulae conniventes (arrows). Air is confined to the small bowel, with no
obvious air in the colon. Note surgical clips from previous surgery. B: An AP
upright film in the same patient as shown in A. Note air-fluid levels in the
small bowel (arrows) with little or no distal bowel gas. C: An AP supine film
of postoperative ileus. Note the presence of air throughout the entire GI
tract.

(Reprinted with permission from Erkonen WE, Smith WL. Radiology 101: The Basics and
Fundamentals of Imaging. Lippincott-Raven, 1998:157, Figure 8-20A and C, and 156, Figure
8-19A.)
```

### Source Excerpt: Intestinal Disorders

_Source lines: 9725-10154._

```text
VI. INTESTINAL DISORDERS
A. Inflammatory bowel disease
1. Crohn disease (regional enteritis)
a. Discussion. Crohn disease is a granulomatous inflammatory condition that
can affect any portion of the digestive tract but most often involves the large
and small bowel. In 87% of patients, the disease involves the terminal ileum.

i. Incidence and prevalence. Men are more frequently affected than women,
and the disease is more common in Jewish people. There are two peaks of
incidence, between the ages of 12 and 30 years and 50 and 60 years.

ii. Etiology. The cause is unclear, but infectious agents, immunologic fac-
tors, and genetic factors have all been implicated.

iii. Pathogenesis. Crohn disease is characterized by areas of transmural
inflammation interspersed with areas of normal bowel. The transmural
nature of the inflammation predisposes to the formation of fistulae and
strictures. Complications include bowel perforation, obstruction, hem-
orrhage, and abscess formation.

b. Clinical features include fever, weight loss, diarrhea (possibly bloody),
and cramping abdominal pain often in the right lower quadrant (terminal
ileum location) that may be worse after meals. Symptoms of complications
may be masked if the patient is taking corticosteroids or narcotic analgesics
(see Clinical Pearl 4-4).

Quick 	HIT
Patients with a history of
antibiotic use within the
preceding 2 weeks should
have a stool sample sent for
a C. difficile toxin assay.

120 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Gastrointestinal Emergencies
c. Differential diagnoses include ulcerative colitis, lymphoma, gastroenteritis,
and appendicitis.

d. Evaluation
i. Known diagnosis. In patients with known Crohn disease, studies should
be performed to rule out complications suggested by the history and
physical examination findings.

a) Laboratory. A CBC may reveal evidence of hemorrhage, infection,
or abscess. Electrolyte levels can be used to assess dehydration and
secondary aberrations.

b) Liver studies are indicated to evaluate suspected biliary pathology.

c) Radiographs. An abdominal series can help rule out obstruction.

A chest radiograph may reveal free air resulting from a perforation.

ii. Suspected diagnosis. In patients who are suspected of having Crohn
disease, a contrast study or colonoscopy may be indicated. These stud-
ies are usually performed on an outpatient basis. To limit radiation,
avoid multiple repeat CT scans and consider bedside ultrasound or mag-
netic resonance imaging scanning instead.

e. Therapy
i. Complications such as perforation or obstruction must be treated with
urgent admission and surgical consultation.

ii. Uncomplicated exacerbations are ideally managed in consultation
with the patient's gastroenterologist. Treatment measures may in-
clude administration of antibiotics (e.g., metronidazole), steroids
(e.g., prednisone; 0.5 mg/kg orally daily), sulfasalazine (3 to 4 g four
times daily), or mesalamine. Biologics such as anti-tumor necrosis
factor infliximab (Remicade, Janssen Biotech, Inc, Horsham, PA) or
immunomodulators such as azathioprine (Imuran, GlaxoSmithKline,
Mississauga, Ontario, Canada) may be options for the patient's gastro-
enterologist to consider.

iii. Severe exacerbations are treated with bowel rest and the intravenous
administration of fluids, antibiotics, and corticosteroids (e.g., hydro-
cortisone; 100 mg every 8 hours or Solu-Medrol) if the patient is cur-
rently taking steroids.

f. Disposition
i. Admission. Indications for admission in patients in whom the diagno-
sis of Crohn disease has been established include:
a) Complications (e.g., obstruction, perforation)
b) Systemic symptoms (e.g., fever, weight loss)
c) Severe symptoms (e.g., pain, symptomatic anemia, bloody diarrhea)
d) Dehydration
ii. Discharge. Patients with a definitive diagnosis who are discharged
require careful instructions to return if the symptoms worsen or compli-
cations develop. Follow-up is essential. Patients in whom the diagnosis
is suspected (on the basis of the history and examination), but who
do not require hospitalization, must be referred to gastroenterology for
follow-up to obtain a definitive diagnosis.

Symptoms of hepatobiliary, renal, rheumatic, ocular, and cutaneous manifestations may be noted
with Crohn disease
1. Biliary abnormalities (e.g., fatty liver, sclerosing cholangitis, cirrhosis, cholelithiasis)
2. Nephrolithiasis (resulting from calcium oxalate stones)
3. Arthritis and ankylosing spondylitis
4. Uveitis and episcleritis
5. Erythema nodosum and pyoderma gangrenosum
PEARL	CLINICAL 	4-4
G A S T R O I N T E S T I N A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	121

Gastrointestinal Emergencies
2. Ulcerative colitis
a. Discussion. Ulcerative colitis is a chronic inflammatory condition affecting
the mucosa and submucosa of the large bowel and rectum.

i. Incidence and prevalence. Women are affected more than men. Peak in-
cidence occurs between the ages of 15 and 30 years and 50 and 65 years.

ii. Pathogenesis. Complications may include perforation, obstruction,
hemorrhage, and toxic megacolon. Toxic megacolon occurs secondary
to the loss of muscle tone in the affected portion of the colon with rapid
dilatation due to gas produced by bacteria.

b. Clinical features of ulcerative colitis include watery diarrhea containing
blood, pus, and mucus; crampy abdominal pain; fever; and weight loss. In a
patient with toxic megacolon, there may be a history of decreased stool output
and the abdomen is distended, tender, and tympanitic. Symptoms or complica-
tions may be masked if the patient is taking steroids or narcotic analgesics.

c. Differential diagnoses include Crohn disease, colon cancer, ischemic colitis,
and diverticulitis.

d. Evaluation
i. Known diagnosis. In patients in whom a diagnosis of ulcerative colitis
has been made, studies should be performed to rule out complications.

a) Radiography. Dilatation of the large bowel to greater than 6 cm is
evident on abdominal films in patients with toxic megacolon.

b) CT scan with wall thickening of 4 mm or greater suggests severe
colitis regardless of cause.

ii. Suspected diagnosis. Patients in whom ulcerative colitis is suspected
require endoscopic evaluation with mucosal biopsies, usually on an out-
patient basis.

e. Therapy
i. Toxic megacolon. Therapeutic measures include admission, strict bowel
rest, serial abdominal exams, administration of intravenous fluids and
electrolytes, administration of broad spectrum antibiotics (e.g., fluoro-
quinolones), and quick look with flexible sigmoidoscopy to assess for
pseudomembranes. Gastrointestinal and surgical consultation is advisable.

ii. Exacerbations are managed similarly as for Crohn disease although re-
sponse to therapies my differ (see VI.A.1.e).

f. Disposition is as for Crohn disease (see VI.A.1.f).

B. Bowel obstruction
1. Discussion. An obstruction of the normal flow of intestinal contents can occur
in either the large or small bowel.

a. Causes include adhesions from prior surgery (the most common cause),
hernias, cancer, inflammatory conditions (e.g., Crohn disease, diverticu-
litis), foreign bodies, volvulus (i.e., twisting of the large bowel around its
mesentery), and intussusception (i.e., invagination of one segment of the
intestine into another).

b. Pathogenesis. Obstruction leads to third spacing of fluid into the bowel wall
and lumen, which can lead to hypovolemia and electrolyte abnormalities. If
the circulation to a segment of the bowel is compromised, the bowel is said
to be strangulated. Strangulation usually results from the twisting of the
bowel on its mesentery and can lead to gangrene, sepsis, and perforation.

2. Clinical features
a. Patient history. The patient may describe obstipation (i.e., a lack of stool or
flatus). Obstipation may not be a clinical feature if the patient presents early
in the course of obstruction or if only a partial obstruction is present.

b. Symptoms
i. The patient complains of cramping epigastric or periumbilical pain
that waxes and wanes .

ii. Vomiting tends to be a prominent feature in patients with a proximal
obstruction or obstruction of the small bowel but may be absent in
patients with a distal colonic obstruction. The vomitus may become bil-
ious and then feculent over time.

Quick 	HIT
Just as in Crohn disease,
extraintestinal manifesta-
tions include sclerosing
cholangitis, uveitis, arthritis,
and aphthous mouth ulcers.

122 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Gastrointestinal Emergencies
c. Physical examination findings
i. The abdomen tends to be diffusely tender and without peritoneal signs.

Peritoneal signs suggest that strangulation or perforation has occurred.

ii. Abdominal distention tends to be more prominent with distal obstructions.

iii. Intermittent, high-pitched bowel sounds may be auscultated, and the
abdomen may be tympanitic to percussion.

3. Differential diagnoses include paralytic ileus (due to electrolyte abnormalities,
trauma, uremia, or peritonitis), cholecystitis, pancreatitis, appendicitis, perfo-
rated peptic ulcer, and mesenteric ischemia.

## 4. Evaluation

a. Laboratory
i. An elevated WBC count with a left shift is suggestive of strangulation.

ii. Electrolytes, blood urea nitrogen, and creatinine levels should be ordered to
rule out an electrolyte abnormality and to further assess hydration status.

iii. The amylase or lipase level may be elevated in the presence of strangu-
lated or gangrenous bowel.

iv. Serum lactate is elevated with advanced ischemia.

b. Abdominal radiographs
i. Distention of the large or small bowel or both may be evident.

a) Valvulae conniventes, which are numerous, narrowly spaced
(%1 mm), and cross the entire lumen, are seen in the small intestine.

b) Haustra, which are less numerous, widely spaced, and do not cross
the entire lumen, are seen in the large intestine. They may become
wider spaced and thickened (thumb printing) with luminal dilata-
tion greater than 6 cm.

ii. Air-fluid levels in a stepladder pattern are consistent with small bowel
obstruction.

iii. A dilated, looped large bowel often pointing to the right side of the ab-
domen is characteristic of a sigmoid volvulus.

iv. A distended colon in the left lower quadrant and absence of right-sided
gas may indicate a cecal volvulus.

v. The "string-of-pearls" sign (i.e., a line of small pockets of air in the
fluid-filled lumen) may be observed.

vi. Free air indicates that perforation has occurred.

c. Other adjunct studies such as an upper gastrointestinal series or diagnos-
tic endoscopies may be required for definitive diagnosis. The abdominal
spiral CT scan is most helpful for determining the point of obstruction and
assessing for the presence of focal bowel wall thickening (colitis) or pneu-
matosis intestinalis (ischemia).

## 5. Therapy

a. Supportive therapy
i. A nasogastric tube should be placed to decompress the bowel proximal
to the obstruction. The patient should be on nothing by mouth orders.

ii. Intravenous fluid hydration is indicated and electrolyte abnormalities should
be corrected. Bladder catheterization may be used to monitor fluid status.

b. Definitive therapy
i. Intussusception is usually diagnosed and treated with a barium or air enema.

ii. Small bowel obstruction may be managed expectantly or with surgical
intervention.

iii. Nonstrangulatory sigmoid volvulus is managed with decompression
and detorsion using a rectal tube.

iv. Cecal volvulus usually requires surgery.

6. Disposition. The patient should be admitted.

C. Diverticular disease
1. Discussion. Diverticula are herniations of the colonic mucosa through the
muscularis propria. They may be located throughout the colon although they
are most frequently found in the sigmoid colon.

a. Incidence. The incidence of diverticula is 30% in patients older than
50 years and increases to 50% in patients 70 years and older.

Quick 	HIT
Right-sided diverticula tend
to present with hemorrhage,
whereas left-sided tend to
present with infection.

G A S T R O I N T E S T I N A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	123

Gastrointestinal Emergencies
b. Etiology and pathogenesis. Diverticula are thought to be caused by a low-
fiber diet, which leads to less stool mass and constipation. The diminished
stool mass causes higher intracolonic pressures, which results in muscular
hypertrophy as the colon attempts to move the stool along. Ultimately, the
elevated intraluminal pressure promotes herniation of the mucosa.

i. Pain is thought to result from the increased muscular contractions
made necessary by the increased intraluminal pressure as well as from
increased gas formation from stagnant bacteria.

ii. Diverticulitis is an inflammatory condition resulting from microperfo-
ration of the diverticulum, which is often caused by feces lodging in the
diverticulum.

iii. Bleeding. Diverticula form near the perforating vessels of the colon.

Bleeding results when the vessel ruptures into the diverticulum. Sev-
enty percent of bleeding diverticula are found in the right colon.

2. Clinical features. Diverticula are most often asymptomatic, but 20% to 30% of
patients eventually manifest some symptoms.

a. Diverticular pain. The pain is a dull, crampy left lower quadrant pain that
is often relieved by the passage of stool or flatus. A firm and tender cord,
representing the hypertrophied sigmoid colon, may be palpable.

b. Diverticulitis
i. Symptoms include left lower quadrant pain that is worse with bowel
movements and may radiate to the back, fever, and, possibly, dysuria
(resulting from bladder irritation).

ii. Physical examination findings. A mass may be noted on palpation of
the left lower quadrant. Rectal examination may reveal a palpable mass
or tenderness.

c. Bleeding. Massive, painless hemorrhage is characteristic.

3. Differential diagnoses
a. Pain and diverticulitis. Differential diagnoses include irritable bowel
syndrome, Crohn disease, ulcerative colitis, gynecologic disorders
(e.g., pelvic inflammatory disease, ovarian torsion), nephrolithiasis, urinary
tract infection, colon cancer, and abdominal aortic aneurysm.

b. Bleeding. Differential diagnoses include arteriovenous malformation, upper
gastrointestinal hemorrhage, hemorrhoids, and cancer.

## 4. Evaluation

a. Pain
b. Diverticulitis
i. Laboratory studies
a) Blood work. A CBC and C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedi-
mentation rate may be helpful. The WBC count is usually increased
with a left shift.

b) Urinalysis may show microscopic hematuria.

ii. Radiography. Abdominal films may demonstrate ileus or obstruction or
a nonspecific bowel gas pattern. A chest radiograph may show free air
in the event of a perforation.

iii. Other studies. A barium enema or abdominal CT scan may be needed
when the diagnosis is not clear.

c. Bleeding diverticula
i. Nasogastric lavage may be performed to rule out an upper gastrointes-
tinal source of hemorrhage.

ii. Anoscopy or sigmoidoscopy can be used to rule out a hemorrhoidal
source of bleeding which also presents as painless hematochezia but
with less blood volume.

iii. Other studies. If the bleeding continues, the patient may require a
tagged RBC study or colonoscopy to verify the source or angiography
with embolization to stop the bleeding. Endoscopy is usually per-
formed electively as an outpatient to confirm the diagnosis and exclude
possible colitis or malignancy.

124 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Gastrointestinal Emergencies

## 5. Therapy

a. Uncomplicated diverticular disease. A high-fiber diet increases stool bulk,
which decreases the intraluminal pressure and relieves the symptoms. An
antispasmodic agent (e.g., dicyclomine) may also be helpful.

b. Diverticulitis. Supportive measures include bowel rest and intravenous
hydration. Antibiotic therapy (e.g., fluoroquinolones, sulfamethoxazole/
trimethoprim, and/or metronidazole) is indicated. Surgery may be necessary
if the patient develops perforation, obstruction, or abscess.

c. Bleeding diverticula
i. Fluid resuscitation. Two large-bore intravenous lines should be placed,
along with a Foley catheter to monitor the resuscitation effort.

ii. Blood products should be administered to maintain a hemoglobin level
of 7 to 8 gm/dL.

iii. Octreotide, 50 #g/hr, may reduce bleeding.

iv. Coagulopathy in the 10% with persistent bleeding should be sought
after and corrected.

v. Angiography or urgent colectomy may be required in the 1% of
patients that fail to respond to more conservative measures.

## 6. Disposition

a. Uncomplicated diverticula. The patient should be carefully instructed to
return if there is a worsening of the pain or the onset of fever. Follow-up
colonoscopy is suggested to verify diagnosis and rule out cancer or colitis.

b. Diverticulitis
i. Patients with mild disease can be treated with oral antibiotics
(trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and metronidazole or fluoroquino-
lones plus metronidazole) on an outpatient basis.

ii. Patients with more severe disease should be admitted for intravenous
antibiotic administration.

iii. Patients with suspected perforation, obstruction, or abscess require
immediate surgical consultation.

c. Bleeding diverticula
i. Patients with significant blood loss or continuing hemorrhage should
be admitted to the intensive care unit.

ii. Patients with stable vital signs and no evidence of continuing bleed-
ing can be admitted to a general medicine unit.
```
